input.task: Summarize why virtual environments matter in MLOps.

content.answer_preview: Virtual environments matter in MLOps because they provide isolation per project, preventing dependency conflicts and global package pollution (Page 29, Chunk 28). This leads to a reproducible setup, where results can be consistently reproduced ("Works on my machine" issue) (Page 29, Chunk 28). Additionally, they allow for pinning specific versions of dependencies to avoid version chaos (Page 29, Chunk 28). Furthermore, virtual environments are created and activated via the command line, which is

final_answer: Virtual environments matter in MLOps because they provide isolation per project, preventing dependency conflicts and global package pollution (Page 29, Chunk 28). This leads to a reproducible setup, where results can be consistently reproduced ("Works on my machine" issue) (Page 29, Chunk 28). Additionally, they allow for pinning specific versions of dependencies to avoid version chaos (Page 29, Chunk 28). Furthermore, virtual environments are created and activated via the command line, which is crucial in MLOps due to its use in Git, CI pipelines, Docker, Kubernetes, and remote server access (Page 13, Chunk 12).
